name: Priests and temple attendants
shortDescription: Priests and temple attendants served the gods in ancient Greece, maintaining temples, performing rituals, and interpreting divine will.
description: In ancient Greece, priests and temple attendants were essential to religious life, acting as intermediaries between humans and the gods. Their roles varied depending on the deity and the specific temple. Generally, they were responsible for maintaining the temple grounds, performing daily rituals, offering sacrifices, and interpreting omens or divine messages. Some priests and priestesses, like the Pythia at Delphi, held significant political influence due to their perceived ability to communicate with the gods. Other attendants managed temple finances, organized festivals, and cared for sacred objects. The selection process for priests and priestesses differed; some positions were hereditary, while others were appointed or chosen by lot. Requirements often included being of noble birth, free from physical blemishes, and possessing a strong moral character. The importance of priests and temple attendants in Greek society is evident in their prominent roles in myths and historical accounts. They ensured the continuity of religious practices and played a crucial part in maintaining the relationship between the mortal and divine realms, influencing decisions from personal matters to state affairs. Their duties were integral to the functioning of ancient Greek society, reinforcing the omnipresence and power of the gods.
